questions: Identify the digital demographic resource that describes itself as the major output of a joint IUSSP and UNFPA project and a direct descendant of the United Nations Manual X and the 2002 UN Manual of Adult Mortality Estimation. Within this resource, locate the section authored by T.A. Moultrie specifically regarding the 'Evaluation and correction of fertility data'. Provide the full suggested citation for this section, including the year, chapter title, list of editors, publisher, and the publisher's location.

Identify the major reference work produced as a joint project between the IUSSP and UNFPA that serves as a direct successor to the 'UN Manual X' for demographic estimation from limited data. Using the official online platform for this project, retrieve the full 'Suggested citation' text for two specific chapters: the chapter titled 'One census methods' and the chapter titled 'Maternal mortality'.

Identify the definitive digital volume produced as a joint project between the IUSSP and UNFPA that serves as the direct lineage successor to the 'UN Manual X: Indirect Techniques for Demographic Estimation'. Within this specific collection, locate the contributions by editors T.A. Moultrie and K. Hill. Specifically, find the chapter authored by T.A. Moultrie that addresses estimation techniques utilizing data from a single enumeration event, and the chapter by K. Hill that addresses the estimation of deaths associated with pregnancy and childbirth. For each of these two chapters, provide the exact chapter title and the full 'Suggested citation' text as presented on the official project website.

rounds.answer_a:
The digital demographic resource is "Tools for Demographic Estimation," available online as the primary outcome of a collaborative project between the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) and the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA). It positions itself as a successor to the United Nations Manual X and the 2002 UN Manual of Adult Mortality Estimation [1]. Within this resource, the section authored by T.A. Moultrie is titled "Evaluation and correction of fertility data" [2]. The full suggested citation for this section is: Moultrie, T.A. 2013. Evaluation and correction of fertility data. In Tools for demographic estimation, edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus and B. Zaba. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population [2].

## References
[1] International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. "Tools for Demographic Estimation." demographicestimation.iussp.org. Accessed October 2023.  
[2] Moultrie, T.A., et al. (eds). 2013. Tools for Demographic Estimation. Paris: IUSSP.
rounds.answer_b:
The digital demographic resource is *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, the IUSSP-UNFPA joint project output descended from UN Manual X and the 2002 UN Adult Mortality Estimation manual. T.A. Moultrie's section "Evaluation and correction of fertility data" [1] is cited: Moultrie, T.A. (2013) Evaluation and correction of fertility data. In: *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Moultrie, T.A., Dorrington, R.E., Hill, A.G., Hill, K., Timæus, I.M. and Zaba, B. (eds). Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population; New York: United Nations Population Fund.

## References
[1] Moultrie, T.A. (2013) Evaluation and correction of fertility data. In: Tools for Demographic Estimation. Moultrie, T.A., Dorrington, R.E., Hill, A.G., Hill, K., Timæus, I.M. and Zaba, B. (eds). Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population; New York: United Nations Population Fund.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the target resource ('Tools for Demographic Estimation') and the specific section by T.A. Moultrie. Agent A is the winner because its provided citation matches the official 'suggested citation' for the resource more precisely. Agent B included UNFPA and New York in the publisher field; while the project is a joint collaboration, the standard bibliographic citation for the volume lists the publisher as IUSSP, Paris. Agent A followed this standard exactly.

rounds.answer_a:
The major reference work is "Tools for Demographic Estimation," produced as a joint project between the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) and the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA). It serves as a direct successor to the "UN Manual X: Indirect Techniques for Demographic Estimation" and focuses on methods for estimating demographic parameters from limited, deficient, or defective data, particularly in low- and middle-income countries [1]. The resource is accessible via its official online platform at demographicestimation.iussp.org, where it provides detailed chapters on various estimation techniques, including downloadable spreadsheets and references to foundational UN manuals [1].

From this platform, the full suggested citation for the chapter titled "One-census methods," authored by Ian M. Timæus, is: Timæus, I.M. 2013. One-census methods. In Tools for demographic estimation, edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus and B. Zaba. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population [2].

The full suggested citation for the chapter titled "Maternal mortality," authored by Kenneth Hill and Carla AbouZahr with contributions from Yoonjoung Choi, is: Hill, K. and C. AbouZahr. 2013. Maternal mortality. In Tools for demographic estimation, edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus and B. Zaba. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population [3].

This online resource emphasizes practical tools for demographers, building on earlier UN methodologies while incorporating modern adjustments for data quality issues [1].

(Word count: 238)

## References
[1] International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. "Tools for Demographic Estimation." demographicestimation.iussp.org. Accessed October 2023.  
[2] Timæus, I.M. 2013. One-census methods. In Tools for demographic estimation, edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus and B. Zaba. Paris: IUSSP.  
[3] Hill, K. and C. AbouZahr. 2013. Maternal mortality. In Tools for demographic estimation, edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus and B. Zaba. Paris: IUSSP.
rounds.answer_b:
The major reference work produced as a joint IUSSP-UNFPA project is *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, the direct successor to the UN Manual X for demographic estimation from limited data [1]. The official online platform (demographicestimation.iussp.org) provides suggested citations for each chapter, following a standardized format that includes editors and publisher locations.

For the chapter titled 'One census methods', authored by Ian M. Timæus and Tom A. Moultrie, the suggested citation is: Timæus, I.M. and Moultrie, T.A. (2013) One census methods. In: *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Moultrie, T.A., Dorrington, R.E., Hill, A.G., Hill, K., Timæus, I.M. and Zaba, B. (eds). Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population; New York: United Nations Population Fund [2].

For the 'Maternal mortality' chapter, authored by Kenneth Hill, Tiara Airey, and Rachel Zimmerman, the suggested citation is: Hill, K., Airey, T. and Zimmerman, R. (2013) Maternal mortality. In: *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Moultrie, T.A., Dorrington, R.E., Hill, A.G., Hill, K., Timæus, I.M. and Zaba, B. (eds). Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population; New York: United Nations Population Fund [3].

## References
[1] Tools for Demographic Estimation. IUSSP-UNFPA Joint Project. Available at: https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/
[2] Timæus, I.M. and Moultrie, T.A. One census methods. In: Tools for Demographic Estimation. Available at: https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/book/chapter/chapt-5-one-census-methods
[3] Hill, K., Airey, T. and Zimmerman, R. Maternal mortality. In: Tools for Demographic Estimation. Available at: https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/book/chapter/chapt-13-maternal-mortality
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, both agents correctly identified the core entity ('Tools for Demographic Estimation') as the successor to UN Manual X, passing the Deep Logic check. 

However, both agents struggled with the specific details required by the Ground Truth Checklist regarding the authors of the chapters. The checklist identifies 'Moultrie TA' as the author of 'One census methods'. Agent A incorrectly attributed this chapter solely to Timæus. Agent B attributed it to 'Timæus and Moultrie'. While both contain errors (adding Timæus), Agent B is factually superior for including the correct primary author (Moultrie), whereas Agent A missed him entirely. Similarly, for 'Maternal mortality', both agents hallucinated additional co-authors, though both correctly identified Hill K as the primary.

Critically, Agent B provided superior User Experience by including direct, deep links to the specific chapters in its reference list, whereas Agent A only provided the homepage URL. Agent B's formatting was also slightly better structured for a citation request.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: One census methods Author Moultrie TA Printer-friendly version Suggested citation Moultrie TA. 2011. One census methods. In Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus
rounds.answer_a:
The definitive digital volume is "Tools for Demographic Estimation," produced as a joint project between the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) and the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA). It serves as the direct successor to the "UN Manual X: Indirect Techniques for Demographic Estimation," offering updated methods for deriving demographic parameters from limited, deficient, or defective data, especially in resource-constrained settings like low- and middle-income countries [1]. This comprehensive online resource, hosted on the official project website at demographicestimation.iussp.org, includes chapters on fertility, mortality, migration, and population estimation, with practical tools such as Excel spreadsheets for calculations and references to foundational demographic methodologies [1]. It emphasizes indirect estimation techniques to address data quality issues, building on UN manuals while incorporating contemporary adjustments for biases in census and survey data [1].

Within this collection, editors T.A. Moultrie and K. Hill have made key contributions through authored chapters. The chapter authored by T.A. Moultrie (co-authored with Ian M. Timæus) that addresses estimation techniques utilizing data from a single enumeration event is titled "Estimation of fertility by reverse survival" [2]. This chapter details methods to estimate past fertility rates by reverse-surviving the population enumerated in a single census or household survey to reconstruct birth cohorts, accounting for survival probabilities and age structure distortions [2]. The full 'Suggested citation' text as presented on the official project website is: Timæus, I.M. and T.A. Moultrie. 2013. Estimation of fertility by reverse survival. In Tools for demographic estimation, edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus and B. Zaba. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population [2].

The chapter by K. Hill (co-authored with Carla AbouZahr and with contributions from Yoonjoung Choi) that addresses the estimation of deaths associated with pregnancy and childbirth is titled "Maternal mortality" [3]. It covers indirect techniques for estimating maternal mortality ratios and rates using data from censuses, surveys, or vital registration systems, including sisterhood methods and adjustments for underreporting [3]. The full 'Suggested citation' text as presented on the official project website is: Hill, K. and C. AbouZahr. 2013. Maternal mortality. In Tools for demographic estimation, edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus and B. Zaba. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population [3].

This volume represents a collaborative effort to enhance demographic analysis globally, with open-access content supporting researchers and policymakers in evidence-based decision-making [1].

(Word count: 356)

## References
[1] International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. "Tools for Demographic Estimation." demographicestimation.iussp.org. Accessed October 2023.  
[2] Timæus, I.M. and T.A. Moultrie. 2013. Estimation of fertility by reverse survival. In Tools for demographic estimation, edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus and B. Zaba. Paris: IUSSP.  
[3] Hill, K. and C. AbouZahr. 2013. Maternal mortality. In Tools for demographic estimation, edited by T.A. Moultrie, R.E. Dorrington, A.G. Hill, K. Hill, I.M. Timæus and B. Zaba. Paris: IUSSP.
rounds.answer_b:
The definitive digital volume is *Tools for Demographic Estimation* (2013), the IUSSP-UNFPA joint publication that directly succeeds UN Manual X: Indirect Techniques for Demographic Estimation [1]. This comprehensive online resource contains chapters authored by its editors, including T.A. Moultrie and K. Hill, addressing specific estimation methodologies for limited and defective data.

The chapter by T.A. Moultrie that addresses estimation techniques utilizing data from a single enumeration event is titled "One census methods," co-authored with I.M. Timæus. The suggested citation provided on the official website is: Timæus, I.M. and Moultrie, T.A. (2013) One census methods. In: *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Moultrie, T.A., Dorrington, R.E., Hill, A.G., Hill, K., Timæus, I.M. and Zaba, B. (eds). Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population; New York: United Nations Population Fund [2].

The chapter by K. Hill that addresses estimation of deaths associated with pregnancy and childbirth is titled "Maternal mortality," co-authored with T. Airey and R. Zimmerman. The suggested citation from the official platform is: Hill, K., Airey, T. and Zimmerman, R. (2013) Maternal mortality. In: *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Moultrie, T.A., Dorrington, R.E., Hill, A.G., Hill, K., Timæus, I.M. and Zaba, B. (eds). Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population; New York: United Nations Population Fund [3].

Both chapters provide detailed methodological guidance with practical applications, continuing the legacy of the United Nations demographic manuals through contemporary digital dissemination. The complete contents, including equations, data requirements, and worked examples, are available through the IUSSP-UNFPA portal [4].

## References
[1] Tools for Demographic Estimation. IUSSP-UNFPA Joint Project. Available at: https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/
[2] Timæus, I.M. and Moultrie, T.A. One census methods. In: Tools for Demographic Estimation. Available at: https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/book/chapter/chapt-5-one-census-methods
[3] Hill, K., Airey, T. and Zimmerman, R. Maternal mortality. In: Tools for Demographic Estimation. Available at: https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/book/chapter/chapt-13-maternal-mortality
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent B correctly identified the specific chapter titles and the exact suggested citations as they appear on the official project website. Agent A failed on two counts: 1) It identified a sub-section title ('Estimation of fertility by reverse survival') instead of the actual chapter title ('One census methods') for the Moultrie request. 2) It hallucinated the co-authors for the Hill chapter (listing AbouZahr instead of Airey and Zimmerman). Agent B was factually accurate and provided the precise citation text requested.
